# Verify `--group-add` / `-G` on `user change-groups` extends the current
# membership, while `--group` still replaces it, and that mixing both flags is
# rejected.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Add bob as a dev.
exec sesam tell --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY --group dev
exec sesam user list
stdout 'bob.*\bdev\b'

# --group-add ops keeps dev and adds ops.
exec sesam user change-groups --user bob --group-add ops
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.change_groups"'
exec sesam log
stdout 'set groups of bob to dev, ops'
exec sesam verify
exec sesam user list
stdout 'bob.*dev,ops'

# The short -G alias behaves the same; re-adding an existing group is a no-op
# union that keeps the membership at dev, ops.
exec sesam user change-groups --user bob -G dev
exec sesam verify
exec sesam user list
stdout 'bob.*dev,ops'

# --group (replace) drops ops again.
exec sesam user change-groups --user bob --group dev
exec sesam verify
exec sesam user list
! stdout '\bops\b'

# Passing both --group and --group-add is a usage error.
! exec sesam user change-groups --user bob --group dev --group-add ops
stderr 'mutually exclusive'
